4 माच(cid:170) 2025 को रा(cid:186)य(cid:332)/संघ रा(cid:186)य (cid:177)े(cid:253)(cid:332)/(cid:214)माट(cid:170) िसटीज िमशन (cid:300)ारा उपल(cid:202)ध कराई गई जानकारी के अनसु ार।
03.04.2025 1257
SUPPORT TO THE PRIVATE ENTERPRISES
5516. SHRI MALVINDER SINGH KANG:
Will the Minister of FOOD PROCESSING INDUSTRIES be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government has any proposal to lure private entrepreneur to
make the food processing industry commercially and economically viable for
domestic and international market and if so, the details thereof; and
(b) the details of the support proposed to be provided by Government to the
private enterprises?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF FOOD PROCESSING
INDUSTRIES; AND MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF RAILWAYS
(SHRI RAVNEET SINGH):
(a) and (b): In order to promote and ensure overall development of Food
Processing Industries, Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI) has been
incentivizing setting up/expansion of related infrastructure through its Central
Sector schemes namely Pradhan Mantri Kisan SAMPADA Yojana (PMKSY)
Scheme, Production Linked Incentive Scheme for Food Processing Industry
(PLISFPI) and Centrally Sponsored – Pradhan Mantri Formalization of Micro
Food Processing Enterprises (PMFME) Scheme across the country. These
schemes are not region or state specific but demand driven.
Under PMKSY, credit linked financial assistance (capital subsidy) is
provided to entrepreneurs for setting up of food processing industries with total
03.04.2025 1258
outlay of Rs 5520 Cr for 15th Finance Commission Cycle. So far, MoFPI has
sanctioned 1608 projects including 41 Mega food Parks, 394 Cold Chain
projects, 75 Agro-processing Clusters projects, 526 Food Processing Units, 61
Creation of Backward and Forward Linkages and 44 Operation Greens projects
under corresponding component schemes of PMKSY across the country till 28th
February, 2025.
Under PMFME scheme, financial, technical and business support is
provided for setting up / upgradation of micro food processing enterprises. The
scheme is operational for a period of upto 2025-26 with an outlay of Rs.10,000
Crore. A total of 1,27,758 micro food processing enterprises have been
approved for assistance under PMFME in the country till 28th February, 2025.
PLISFPI is, inter alia, intended to support creation of global food
manufacturing champions and support Indian brands of food products in
international market. The Scheme is operational for a period from 2021-22 to
2026-27 with an outlay of Rs. 10,900 crores. So far, 171 proposals from 133
food processing companies have been approved for assistance under various
categories of the PLISFPI scheme in the country till 28th February, 2025.
Project Execution Agency (PEA) / Implementing agencies /organizations
such as Govt. / PSUs / Joint Ventures / NGOs / Cooperatives / Self Help Groups
(SHGs) / Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) / Farmer Producer Companies
(FPCs) / Private Sector companies/ Partnership firm / Proprietorship
firms/individuals etc. are eligible for availing grants under these schemes of
MoFPI.
03.04.2025 1259
Apart from this, with the objective to boost investments and sourcing of
Indian food products, MoFPI organized 3rd edition of a Mega Event, called
“World Food India” during 19th to 22nd September 2024 at Bharat Mandapam,
New Delhi to showcase domestic Industry and provide it with collaborative
opportunities with international stakeholders. The event brought global food
processing companies, innovators, supply chain stakeholders, equipment
manufacturers, etc. on a collaborative platform and provide foreign companies
with tie-up/ business opportunities with its Indian counterparts.
The details of incentives available under PMKSY, PLISFPI and PMFME
schemes of MoFPI are at Statement.
03.04.2025 1260
STATEMENT

03.04.2025 1262
Incentives available under Production Linked Incentive Scheme for Food
Processing Industry (PLIS-FPI)
i. The beneficiary should achieve minimum year on year sales growth of 10%
for claiming incentive under Category-I, Category-II and Millet-Based Products
components of the Scheme. Under Category -I component, the companies have
to make committed investments to increase their production capacities. If a
company does not make the committed investment upto end of 2023-24, it is not
eligible to receive incentives under the Scheme.
ii. Under Category-III, i.e., Branding and Marketing component, a company is
eligible for financial incentives @ 50% of expenditure incurred on Branding and
Marketing abroad subject to a maximum of 3% of Sales of food products or Rs 50
crore per year, whichever is less. The minimum expenditure should be Rs. 5
crore over a period of five years.
The details of assistance available to Micro Food Processing Enterprises
under PMFME Scheme
(i). Support to Individual / Group Category Micro Enterprises: Credit-linked capital
subsidy @35% of the eligible project cost, maximum ceiling Rs.10 lakh per unit;
(ii). Support to SHGs for seed capital: Seed capital @ Rs. 40,000/- per member of
SHG engaged in food processing for working capital and purchase of small tools
subject to maximum of Rs. 4 lakh per SHG Federation.
(iii). Support for Common Infrastructure: Credit linked capital subsidy @35%
subject to maximum of Rs. 3 crore to support FPOs, SHGs, Cooperatives and
any Government agency for setting up of common infrastructure. The common
03.04.2025 1263
infrastructure will also be available for other units and public to utilize on hiring
basis for substantial part of the capacity.
(iv). Branding and Marketing Support: Grant upto 50% for Branding and
Marketing to groups of FPOs/ SHGs/ Cooperatives or an SPV of micro food
processing enterprises.
(v). Capacity Building: The scheme envisages training for Entrepreneurship
Development Skilling (EDP+): program modified to meet the requirement of food
processing industry and product specific skilling.
VEHICLE SCRAPPING
5517. SHRI GURMEET SINGH MEET HAYER:
Will the Minister of ROAD TRANSPORT AND HIGHWAYS be pleased to state:
(a) the manner in which the Government is planning to integrate traditional scrap
dealers into the Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility (RVSF) system to ensure
their participation and livelihood security given that the existing network of
traditional scrap dealers operates in a labour-intensive manner while the RVSF
framework is more technology-driven;
(b) whether there are any training programmes, financial incentives or skill
development initiatives to help scrap labourers transition to the new system and if
so, the details thereof;
03.04.2025 1264
(c) the measures being taken to authorise traditional scrap dealers to issue valid
scrapping certificates for availing new vehicle purchase incentives;
(d) the manner in which the Government is planning to regulate and standardize
scrap collection while preventing illegal or unregistered vehicle scrapping;
(e) whether the Union Government collaborates with the State Governments or
private players to support infrastructure development for small scrap dealers and
if so, the details thereof; and
(f) the steps being taken to prevent environmental hazards from unregulated
scrapping and ensure compliance with waste disposal norms?
THE MINISTER OF ROAD TRANSPORT AND HIGHWAYS (SHRI NITIN
JAIRAM GADKARI):
(a) and (b): (i) The Vehicle Scrapping Policy aims to reduce pollution from old and
unfit vehicles in an environment friendly manner through a scientific scrapping
process. Government in Ministry of Road Transport and Highways has notified
the Motor Vehicles (Registration and Functions of Vehicle Scrapping Facility)
Rules, 2021 vide GSR 653(E) dated 23.09.2021 (further amended vide GSR 695
(E) dated 13.09.2022 and GSR 212 (E) dated 15.03.2024).
(ii) These rules provides for the integration of unorganised/informal sector with
the formal scrapping ecosystem. Further, sub-rule 12 of rule 13 of these rules
provides that a Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility (RVSF) may establish any
collection centre at any place, other than the Scrapping Yard and, if collection
03.04.2025 1265
Centre undertakes activities such as, depollution and dismantling, then, the
requirements applicable for a Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility shall also be
applicable to such Collection Centre.
(c): As per Rule 3 of the Motor Vehicles (Registration and Functions of Vehicle
Scrapping Facility) Rules, 2021, Certificate of Deposit (CD) means the certificate
issued by the Registered Vehicle Scrapping Facility (RVSF) to recognise the
submission of the vehicle from the registered owner to the said RVSF for further
treatment and, Certificate of Vehicle Scrapping (CVS) means the certificate
issued by a RVSF to recognise the final disposal of a vehicle. The CD and CVS
can be issued by RVSFs only.
(d): In order to prevent illegal or unregistered vehicle scrapping, following
provisions are provided in the Motor Vehicles (Registration and Functions of
Vehicle Scrapping Facility) Rules, 2021:
(i) Under Rule 9, the registration authority is duly authorised for inspection of the
RVSFs. The registration authority can order to cancel or suspend the license of
the facility in case of non-compliance, after following due process.
(ii) Rule 11 provides that the RVSF, after completing the necessary treatment,
shall issue a digital Certificate of Vehicle Scrapping to update the VAHAN
Database and inform the competent authority of the State Government or Union
territory Administration for updating of records of the vehicle.
03.04.2025 1266
(iii) Rule 14 mandates timely audit of RVSFs by any of the agencies specified
under rule 126 of the Central Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989.
(e): In order to showcase business opportunities to the investors, Investor
summits have been conducted in collaboration with various State Governments.
Further, Government also provides necessary support to the investors for
establishing a RVSF by conducting meetings, consultations, etc.
(f): As per sub-rule (xix) of rule 10 of the Motor Vehicles (Registration and
Functions of Vehicle Scrapping Facility) Rules, 2021, the Registered Vehicle
Scrapping Facility shall ensure that removal or re-cycling or disposal of
hazardous parts of the scrapped vehicle is done as per the guidelines issued by
the Central Pollution Control Board for environmentally sound management of
End-of-Life Vehicles and AIS-129.

IMPLEMENTATION OF ABY IN NALGONDA DISTRICT OF TELANGANA
5519. SHRI KUNDURU RAGHUVEER:
Will the Minister of JAL SHAKTI be pleased to state:
(a) whether the Government is aware of the groundwater crisis in Nalgonda
district, Telangana and if so, the details thereof;
(b) whether the Atal Bhujal Yojana (ABY) is being implemented in Nalgonda
district and if so, the details of the blocks/villages covered under ABY;
(c) the steps taken/being taken by the Government to ensure community
participation and sustainable groundwater management in Nalgonda under ABY;
(d) the details of the funds allocated and utilized for the implementation of ABY in
Nalgonda district so far; and
(e) the measures taken/being taken to address the challenges faced in the
implementation of the scheme in Telangana, particularly in Nalgonda district?
THE MINISTER OF STATE IN THE MINISTRY OF JAL SHAKTI (DR. RAJ
BHUSHAN CHOUDHARY):
03.04.2025 1274
(a): Assessment of Dynamic Ground Water Resources of each State/UT is being
annually carried out jointly by Central Ground Water Board (CGWB) and
concerned State Nodal/Ground Water Departments. As per the latest
Assessment of 2024, the Total Annual Ground Water Recharge for Nalgonda
district is 1.21 Billion Cubic Meter (BCM) and Total Annual Ground Water
Extraction for all uses has been estimated as 0.572 Billion Cubic Meter (Bcm).
The Stage of Ground Water Extraction (SoE), which is a measure of Annual
Ground Water Extraction over Annual Extractable Ground Water Resource is
arrived at 52.35% for the district as a whole, indicating that the district is in ‘Safe’
category overall. Further, out of the total 33 Assessment Units (Mandals) in the
district, 25 units have been categorized as ‘Safe’ and only 2 units have been
categorized as ‘Over-exploited’.
In addition to the above, CGWB also monitors groundwater levels
throughout the country including Nalgonda District of Telangana, four times in
every year. Perusal of ground water level data of Nalgonda, monitored during
November, 2024, indicates that about 95% of monitored wells are showing water
levels in the range of 0 – 10 mbgl (meters below ground level) indicating ease of
access to ground water.
Further, in order to assess the long term fluctuation in ground water level in
Nalgonda District, the water level data collected by CGWB during November
2024 has been compared with the decadal mean of November (2014-2023). Such
03.04.2025 1275
analysis of water level data indicates that about 65% of the wells monitored have
registered rise in ground water levels.
(b) to (e) :Atal Bhujal Yojana is not being implemented in the State of Telangana.

12.03½ hrs
MESSAGES FROM RAJYA SABHA
SECRETARY GENERAL: Sir, I have to report the following message received
from the Secretary General of Rajya Sabha:
“In accordance with the provisions of rule 115 of the Rules of
Procedure and Conduct of Business in the Rajya Sabha, I am
directed to inform the Lok Sabha that the Rajya Sabha, at its sitting
held on the 2nd April, 2025, agreed to the following amendments
made by the Lok Sabha at its sitting held on the 25th March, 2025, in
the Boilers Bill, 2024:-
ENACTING FORMULA
1. That at page 1, line 1, for the words “Seventy-fifth”, the words
“Seventy-sixth” be substituted.
03.04.2025 1288
CLAUSE 1
2. That at page 1, line 5, for the figure “2024”, the figure “2025” be
substituted.”
I also have to report the following message received from the Secretary
General of Rajya Sabha:
“In accordance with the provisions of rule 127 of the Rules of
Procedure and Conduct of Business in the Rajya Sabha, I am
directed to inform the Lok Sabha that the Rajya Sabha at its
sitting held on the 2nd April, 2025, agreed without any
amendment to the Immigration and Foreigners Bill, 2025,
which was passed by the Lok Sabha at its sitting held on the
27th March, 2025.”

SHRI BHARTRUHARI MAHTAB (CUTTACK): Sir, for ages Mahanadi is the
only river in Odisha which has a perennial flow of water. However, due to
construction of some large barrages in the river's catchment areas in
neighbouring Chhattisgarh, the flow of water in Mahanadi has been adversely
affected. Water levels in Odisha's Hirakud Dam are going down posing water
crisis in downstream regions. The crisis is alarming as Mahanadi is almost dry
two weeks before the month of Baishak. Our concern lies in having water during
non-monsoon times as well. The river is exposed to severe erosion. Sand and Silt
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Odiya.
03.04.2025 1292
too are a bane. The bed of the river is rising due to sand formation and
endangering the embankments. The river's earlier capacity of handling 9 lakh
cusecs of water has been upgraded to 11 lakh cusecs.
All embankments are in dangerous condition. If floods happen, then
embankments will collapse. So, I request the central government for dredging the
riverbeds. The Centre and the state government should jointly carry out the
dredging in Mahanadi and its subsidiary rivers as soon as possible, so that
Odisha can be saved from floods.
SHRI VIJAYAKUMAR ALIAS VIJAY VASANTH (KANYAKUMARI): Hon.
Speaker, Sir, I bring to your attention a pressing matter that affects the livelihoods
of millions of Indians.
The recent RBI rule change requiring full repayment of gold loans along
with interest after 12 months has imposed a significant financial burden on
vulnerable sections of our society, including farmers, small business owners,
fishermen, and low-income individuals. The gold loans have been a vital source
of financing for these individuals, providing quick liquidity during critical times.
However, the new rule has taken away the flexibility to renew loans by paying
only the interest, forcing them to seek help from informal lenders who charge
exorbitant interest rates.
To address this issue, I propose the following: There should be jewel loans
with easy interest payment and renewal options; education loans should be given
without collateral security as most of the banks are asking for collateral security;
and hassle-free loans for MSMEs should also be given without collateral security.
03.04.2025 1293
The RBI’s recent changes to the gold loan rules aim to promote a more
transparent and secure gold lending ecosystem. However, we must ensure that
these changes do not inadvertently harm the common man.
I request the Government to reconsider the recent RBI rule change and
provide relief to the vulnerable section of our society. Thank you.

(cid:174)ी छोटेलाल (राबट्(cid:170)सगंज) : सर, हमार े संसदीय (cid:177)े(cid:253) रॉबट्(cid:170)सगंज सोनभ(cid:254) के द(cid:294)ु ी िवधानसभा, सदर

आिदवािसय(cid:332) को वनािधकार के तहत िजतना प(cid:280)ा िमलना चािहए, उतना नह(cid:233) िमल रहा ह ै । उनके
बाप-दादा के जमान े स,े ए(cid:214)टेट राजा के जमान े स े जो जमीन िमली हई थी चिकया िवधानसभा म(cid:164), वह
जमीन दिलत, िपछड़,े आिदवािसय(cid:332) व िकसान(cid:332) स े छीनी जा रही ह ै ।

SHRI N. K. PREMACHANDRAN (KOLLAM): Sir, my ‘Zero Hour’ submission is
relating to construction of an underpass at Vettuthara junction on National
Highway-66 in my constituency.
Sir, Vettuthara is an important junction which is connecting the eastern part
03.04.2025 1297
of my constituency to National Highway-66 covering Thekkumbhagam gram
panchayat, Thevalakkara gram panchayat and so many other places in the
eastern part. Those people are dependent on this route. They connect to the
National Highway through Vettuthara junction. Unfortunately, there is no vehicular
underpass at that particular junction.
And further, as far as the service roads are concerned, it was originally
planned to have seven metres of width of the service road, but unfortunately, the
width has come down to four metres, which has made the movement very difficult
for the residents of the eastern part of that junction. Hundreds of vehicles are
coming from the eastern part to get to the National Highway-66, but there is no
underpass leading to difficulties. I learned that there is a proposal to construct an
underpass with the width of 22.9 metres near the Neendakara Bridge. My
suggestion is, either a new vehicular underpass be constructed at Vettuthara
junction or the newly proposed underpass with 22.9 metre width be shifted to
Vettuthara junction to resolve this problem. I would urge the Government to do it
at the earliest for the convenience of the people at large.

SHRI ESWARASAMY K. (POLLACHI): Hon Chairman Sir, I wholeheartedly
thank you for allowing me to raise an important issue pertaining to my Pollachi
parliamentary constituency. The National Highway no 209 between Dindigul and
Kamalapuram is being constructed through Puliampatti, Kittasurampalayam
Kurumbapalayam and Achipatti. Since this National Highway is being laid at an
elevated level of 20 feet high for a stretch of 3 kilometres, people are unable to
reach their agricultural fields. Particularly the farmers who have given their lands
for this Highway project are facing the difficulties. In the absence of approach
roads, farmers are suffering a lot as they are unable to transport the agricultural
produce grown in their fields. As a result the livelihood of farmers are very much
affected in this area. On behalf of the villagers of this area, I make a fervent
appeal to the Union Government that these approach roads should be
constructed at the earliest without any difficulty to the people and the vehicular
traffic of this area. Further, as this area is near the Pollachi Municipality, it is
included in the model list of areas which are identified for expansion of
Municipality jurisdiction. As these villages are growing fast, I urge that these
approach roads should be constructed on both the sides of the Highway at the
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
03.04.2025 1301
actual ground level in order to help the farmers and general public of this area.
Moreover I urge that permanent road should be constructed near the
Kittasurampalayam bus terminus, for the transport of Light Motor Vehicles.
Vedappatti village near Madathukulam has become like an isolated island due to
non-availability of over-bridge and underpass. I urge that over-bridge and
underpass should be constructed in this area. Along the Pollachi-Dindigul
National Highway, between Marappettai to Unjavelampatti, several accidents take
place resulting in loss of lives due to the median constructed on this stretch of the
Highway. We have requested the National Highways Project Directorate to
remove this median on this road. I on behalf of the people of my Pollachi
parliamentary Constituency once again request for removal of this Median along
the Marappettai-Unjavelampatti stretch of Pollachi-Dindigul National Highway in
order to prevent loss of precious lives due to accidents. Thank you Vanakkam.

SHRIMATI DIMPLE YADAV (MAINPURI): Thank you, hon. Speaker, Sir.
I would like to speak on a very important matter, and I believe that most of
the Members of Parliament sitting in the House -- now, not present in the House -
- are going to align with it. It is regarding the stepmotherly and unethical treatment
being meted out to the ex-servicemen under the Ex-Servicemen Contributory
Health Scheme.
03.04.2025 1306
I am given to understand that presently the scheme is facing a shortfall of
more than Rs. 5000 crore. Moreover, since September, 2023, the fund allotment
to the scheme has been erratic and scarce compared to the projected
requirements.
The Central Government Health Scheme (CGHS) in spite of having a high
per capita expenditure does not fix any issues and continues to be well-funded
which is much appreciated but at the same time, this is leading to humiliation and
discrimination against the ECHS patients in empanelled hospitals. At times, if in
one family or in one friend circle or community, there are CGHS, ECHS, the State
ESI and PSU members and when all of them approach the same empanelled
hospital, the ECHS members are subtly turned away stating there is a lack of
beds whereas others continue to be attended to.
In addition to this, the trauma of the illness and the financial distress is
causing the proud soldier to face loss in the society and in the long run, it will be
detrimental to the image of the soldiers of the nation as a whole.
Though this discrimination in fund allotment has been going on earlier also,
this year, it has been monumental. The empanelled hospitals are forced to close
the financial year due to the shortfall in each ECHS payments. In the long run,
these hospitals will not be empanelled with ECHS again.
Most cancer and expensive medicines are not available in the ECHS clinics
and the soldiers are being given non-availability certificates to buy from the
market and they are told to claim this money and when these soldiers apply for
reimbursement of money, this claim is not passed as they have no funds with the
03.04.2025 1307
ECHS.
Sir, I would like the Ministry to take immediate steps regarding the release
of complete requirement of ECHS funds so that the empanelled facilities can
continue. At the same time, I would request the hon. Minister to look into the
matter and to do the needful. The soldiers have given the prime of their life to the
nation and I being the daughter of an ex-serviceman also, I understand their
plight and their situation which these soldiers are facing. I do not want these
soldiers to be humiliated and discriminated in old age.
Thank you, Sir.

SHRI T. R. BAALU (SRIPERUMBUDUR): Sir, yesterday, the State Assembly of
Tamil Nadu has adopted a Special Resolution moved by the hon. Chief Minister
of Tamil Nadu, Thalapathy Thiru M.K. Stalin, regarding the unconstitutional
ceding of Indian territory, Katchatheevu, to Sri Lanka.
Sir, why am I saying it an unconstitutional move? In 1974, there was an
International Maritime Boundary Agreement between India and Sri Lanka. In
1976, there was one more agreement between India and Sri Lanka regarding
demarcation of specific number of points on the maritime boundary. The State of
Tamil Nadu was not a party to both these agreements.
Finally, before I say anything, I would also like to mention the Supreme
Court Order of 1960 which dealt with a territorial dispute between India and
Pakistan over the Berubari region. In that case the Supreme Court clearly held
that ceding of any land territory should not be done without the approval from the
Parliament. The Parliament will have to ratify it. But ceding of Katchatheevu
happened in 1976 during the imposition of the President's Rule. At that time, they
did not consult the State Government. They did not consult the Parliament. This
was done without any ratification of the Parliament. That is why, I mentioned that
is unconstitutional. ....(Interruptions)
Sir, ceding of Katchatheevu, the sole breadwinner for fishermen of Tamil
Nadu, is still unconstitutional. It should be retrieved by the hon. Minister who is
sitting there. They should intimate the Prime Minister of India or the Minister of
External Affairs so that it can happen quickly. Otherwise, the agreement is still
unconstitutional. There is no Parliament sanction to it. ....(Interruptions) There is
03.04.2025 1313
no ratification by the Parliament of India. Therefore, I request the Government of
India to retrieve it as early as possible. Thank you.

SHRI BENNY BEHANAN (CHALAKUDY): India, with the world’s largest youth
population, is grappling with an alarming surge in drug abuse and violent crimes.
Mafia networks are actively trapping young minds, infiltrating colleges and
educational institutions to expand their influence. The illusion of social media and
the glorification of violence in films are distorting reality, normalising crime and
aggression among the youth. Meanwhile, unchecked drug trafficking networks
continue to spread, poisoning an entire generation.
Kerala is at a breaking point. In the last two months, many brutal murders
have been reported. Political backing and societal tolerance for criminals have
emboldened lawlessness, pushing society to the brink of chaos.
03.04.2025 1315
The drug menace is no longer confined to urban centres, it has infiltrated schools,
colleges, and even rural areas, affecting families across all social strata. This is
no longer just a law enforcement issue; it is a national crisis. Awareness alone
would not work. The Government must act decisively with strict enforcement,
harsher punishments, and a zero-tolerance policy toward drug peddlers and
violent offenders. Strengthening border security, enhancing intelligence networks,
and launching nationwide crackdowns on drug syndicates are crucial. Educational
institutions must be safeguarded from becoming hubs of drug circulation. A
dedicated task force should be formed to combat the growing drug crisis with
Central and State coordination. The Central Government must prioritise this crisis
with urgent policy interventions and swift, decisive action.
Thank you, Sir.

clearance for the film and the film was released. But a systematic attack has
happened on the actors, producers and directors of the film. Now, they are forced

03.04.2025 1317
Actually, it is a shame for the nation that after the Censor Board gave the

SHRI MALAIYARASAN D. (KALLAKURICHI): Hon. Madam Chairperson,
Vanakkam. More than 2 lakh tribal people live in the surrounding hilly areas such
as Yercaud, Pachaimalai Neyyamalai, Aranoothumalai, Kalvarayanmalai,
Vellimalai and Dharmadurai of my Kallakurichi parliamentary constituency. They
belong to the most backward areas. They are living a simple life. Our Union
Government is giving houses to them under the Prime Minister’s Housing
Scheme, PMAY. Amount provided under this Scheme for construction of houses
is very less. An amount of Rs. 2,78,460 is being provided to each beneficiary. The
expenses are doubled as regards the procurement of the raw material and
construction material for these tribal people. Therefore, keeping in view of this
surge in material cost, I urge that the amount disbursed under PMAY should be
enhanced for the tribal people of my area. The Adidravida and Tribal People
Welfare Department of Tamil Nadu under the able guidance of Hon Chief Minister
of Tamil Nadu is providing Rs. 5,35,140 for construction of houses by these tribal
people. I wish to make a mention about this here. I urge that a similar enhanced
amount should be provided by the Union Government for construction of houses
by these tribal people under PMAY.
Thank you. Vanakkam.

SUSHRI S. JOTHIMANI (KARUR): Hon. Chairperson, Madam, I rise today to
highlight an urgent issue concerning consumer safety. Recently, the Bureau of
Indian Standards raided warehouses of e-commerce giants Amazon and Flipkart
in Tiruvallur, Tamil Nadu and Delhi. They seized thousands of products worth
over Rs. 1 crore being sold without mandatory BIS certification or with fake ISI
mark. These included everyday household items like stainless bottles, geysers,
food mixers and sports items. Sub-standard products can pose serious health
and safety risk. This is not an isolated incident but a growing and alarming trend.
E-commerce platforms dominate the online market space, yet many of them
continue to allow uncertified products to be sold, misleading customers who trust
these platforms for quality and safety. While the BIS Act prescribes penalties, its
enforcement happens only after raids. By then, many unsafe products have
already reached homes across India.
03.04.2025 1322
I urge the hon. Minister to strengthen BIS enforcement to prevent the sale
of uncertified goods at the source, promote the BIS mark so that consumers can
verify certification before purchase, hold e-commerce platforms accountable,
ensuring that they list only BIS certified products, strengthen the legal framework
to ensure that no company is able to escape from the consumer products’ quality
and standard.

SHRI RAJMOHAN UNNITHAN (KASARAGOD): Thank you very much, Madam.
I would like to draw your attention to the distressing situation involving seven
Indian seafarers whom pirates at sea have kidnapped. Among them is Shri
Rajeendran Bhargavan from my constituency, Kasaragod, along with another
individual from Kochi, Kerala. The other five seafarers are from a different part of
India. This incident occurred aboard the ship Bitu River, managed by Maritec
Tanker Management Pvt. Ltd.
The journey began on September 6, 2024, and the last contact was made
on March 17, 2025. On March 18, the families were informed of the hijacking. Shri
03.04.2025 1324
Rajeendran’s family, hailing from a poor fishing community, is in severe distress.
His wife, who is in the advanced stages of pregnancy, is living in fear and
uncertainty, without any updates on his safety. The families of the other
kidnapped crew members are also experiencing similar anguish.
This matter is of national concern. Therefore, I urge upon the Government
of India to take immediate and decisive action to locate and rescue these seven
Indians and provide relief to these families and ensure the safety of our Indian
seafarers.

SHRI SUKANTA KUMAR PANIGRAHI (KANDHAMAL): Hon. Chairperson,
Madam, I wish to bring to the attention of the Government the rich yet neglected
Buddhist heritage of Boudh District of Odisha. The very name Boudh signifies its
historical connection with Lord Buddha and Buddhism. Yet, the region has not
received due recognition and support for its preservation and promotion.
Archaeological findings including a large image of Lord Buddha in
Bhumisparsha Mudra, ancient monastic remains, pillars, bricks, and votive
stupas, indicate that Boudh was once a flourishing centre of Buddhism.
Additionally, Dambarugarh was a known hub of Tantric Buddhism, where
scholars wrote influential Buddhist scripts. Despite these discoveries, Boudh
remains out of India’s officially-recognised Buddhist Circuit.
I urge the hon. Minister of Culture and Tourism to take the following steps,
which include, to direct the Archaeological Survey of India to conduct a
comprehensive study … (Interruptions)

SHRI K. E. PRAKASH (ERODE): Hon. Madam Chairperson, Vanakkam. Indian
Institute of Technology, IIT is situated in Chennai and the National Institute of
Technology, NIT is situated in Tiruchy. These are the premier educational
instructions imparting Engineering Education. In order to get access to the world
class education available in IIT, Chennai, students of my Erode constituency have
to travel long for 400 kilometers facing so many hardships. I therefore urge upon
that the institutions of higher learning such as IIT and NIT should be set up in the
educational district of Erode, in the western region of Tamil Nadu, which has large
number of aspiring students of Engineering. Erode is one among the fast-

developing Cities of Tamil Nadu and under the able Government led by Hon Chief

secondary education in Government Schools. As the Tamil Nadu government is
giving more importance to education, the State is fast developing in the field of

CAPTAIN VIRIATO FERNANDES (SOUTH GOA): Thank you, Madam. Today, I
rise to bring to the notice of the House a unique and historically significant form of
theatre called ‘Tiatr’, which is in existence for 133 years. Now, Tiatr is the only
popular theatre form in Asia which is truly democratic, secular, community-
oriented, and independent of elite and feudal influences. I request the Ministry of
Culture, Government of India, to take immediate steps to initiate and support the
process of seeking UNESCO’s Intangible Cultural Heritage inscription for this
Tiatr.
I would like to place a few demands before the Ministry of Culture. First,
recognise Tiatr as a nationally important cultural tradition and take all necessary
measures to ensure its promotion, conservation, and documentation. Secondly,
establish a task force committee under the Ministry of Culture in collaboration with
the Government of Goa and representatives from the Tiatr community to facilitate
the process of UNESCO inscription. Thirdly, encourage national and international
research, academic collaboration, and digital archiving of theatre performances to
preserve their historical, artistic, and cultural significance.

DR. BACHHAV SHOBHA DINESH (DHULE): Hon. Chairperson, I would like to
draw this august House’s attention towards a demand to construct a Tribal
Museum at Baghlan in my Lok Sabha Constituency, Dhule. There are more than
150 villages in Baghlan Tehsil and traditional forms of arts, handicrafts, Paintings,
Warli art and culture etc. have been conserved and protected by the local tribal
community. It would help to keep this tribal culture alive and preserve the cultural
heritage. Mangitungi is also a famous pilgrimage centre for Jain community. A
108 feet height statue of Shri Rishabhdev is also erected there and Salher-Mulher
fort of Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj is also located in this area. It is also
recognized as a proposed UNESCO world Heritage Site.
It would definitely boost tourism in this region and help for employment
generation. It is also beneficial for the local artists, artisans and traders.
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
03.04.2025 1332
Therefore, I request Hon. Tribal Affairs Minister and Culture Minister to
provide sufficient funds for the construction of this Tribal Museum so that it can
help to preserve the cultural heritage of tribals there. It is surely going to help the
tribals not only from my State Maharashtra, but also the tribals living in other
States of our country.
Thank you.

SHRI YADUVEER WADIYAR (MYSORE): Thank you very much, Madam, for
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Bengali.
03.04.2025 1333
allowing me to bring an issue of national importance to the attention of this august
House. It concerns our Bandipur Tiger Reserve.
Madam, in 2009, the Karnataka Government implemented a night ban over
NH-766 which runs through Chamarajanagar district in Karnataka and connects
to Wayanad in Kerala. Despite this progressive policy which was put into place to
ensure that wildlife collisions are at a minimum, and also that the illegal activity
that happens during the night time is also at a minimum, the Kerala Government
has repeatedly sought to lift this ban. Time and again, they have approached the
Karnataka Government. The sitting Chief Minister, who was also the Chief
Minister in his previous term, had denied the lifting of the night ban twice. Despite
this, recently an affidavit was submitted to the Supreme Court which advocated
for an elevated road on an alternative route and keeping NH-766 only for local
community use and emergency vehicles. The senior leaders within the Congress
have exerted pressure on the Karnataka Government to reconsider lifting the ban
and also take back the affidavit that was filed in the Supreme Court.
Madam, such interference in Karnataka's affairs, which undermines the
State's efforts to protect its natural heritage for the benefit of a few political figures
is deeply condemnable. I respectfully urge you to direct through this House the
Ministry concerned to take a firm stance regarding safeguarding our tiger
reserves and ensuring that they are not compromised for the political interests of
a select few. Thank you.

SHRI OMPRAKASH BHUPALSINH ALIAS PAVAN RAJENIMBALKAR
(OSMANABAD): Hon. Chairperson, I would like to draw the attention of this
august House towards an important issue which is related to the young and
unemployed youth of my constituency. My district is at 3rd position in the list of
most backward districts of our country. Around 310 hectare land of Kawadgaon
MIDC and 89 hectare land of Wadgaon MIDC is lying vacant.
One new MIDC is proposed at Tamalwadi and Shiradon. Madam, through
you, I would like to request Central Govt. to develop an industrial cluster there so
that the local youths would get employment opportunities at the same place. If
the industries related to Terkheda crackers market, leather market at Yedki, Khoa
market at Bhoom Paranda and Osmanabadi goats, get developed in my
Constituency, it would be very beneficial.
Therefore, I request you to start and develop the industry which consume
less water to empower the unemployed youth of my constituency.
Thank you.

SHRI KODIKUNNIL SURESH (MAVELIKKARA): Madam Chairperson, I am
raising a very urgent and important matter regarding the incident which has
happened in Jabalpur, Madhya Pradesh, where Catholic priests and laity were
brutally attacked by Hindutva extremists.
On 1st April, 2025, a group of Catholic pilgrims from Mandla parish, who
were peacefully participating in Jubilee 2025 religious observances, were
03.04.2025 1337
unlawfully detained twice by extremist elements and subjected to violence.
Notably, when senior priests, including the Vicar General of Jabalpur, arrived to
support the victims, they too were assaulted. Such brazen attacks on religious
minorities not only violate constitutional rights but also expose the growing
climate of intolerance and impunity for extremist violence.
Despite claims of disassociation by the RSS, the incident raises serious
concerns about law and order, religious freedom, and the safety of citizens
practising their faith. The Government must take urgent and decisive action to
ensure swift and impartial action against the perpetrators.
Thank you, Madam.

DR. GANAPATHY RAJKUMAR P. (COIMBATORE): Hon Madam Chairperson,
Vanakkam. I thank you for allowing me to raise an important issue pertaining to
the workers of cotton mills. There are seven NTC cotton mills in Tamil Nadu. Out
of these seven, five NTC mills are in Coimbatore and one each in Sivagangai and
Ramanathapuram. There are 4000 workers in these NTC mills. After the Covid-19
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
03.04.2025 1344
lockdown of the year 2020, full salary was provided to the workers of these NTC
cotton mills up to 17.5.2020. Thereafter 50 per cent of the salary amount was
provided to these workers. After 5 October 2024, the disbursal of salary to these
workers was completely stopped. Workers are in deep distress after losing their
livelihood. I urge upon the Union Minister of Textiles to take necessary and swift
action for making these NTC mills functional and light the lamp in the lives of the
workers of these cotton mills. Thank you.

SHRI HAMDULLAH SAYEED (LAKSHADWEEP): Madam, thank you for giving
me an opportunity to speak. I would like to draw the attention of the Government
to the critical situation concerning seven Indian seafarers aboard the Panama-
flagged vessel MV Bitu River, which was attacked by armed pirates off the coast
of Sao Tomé and Príncipe on 18th March 2025. Among those affected is young
Asif Ali from Minicoy Island, Lakshadweep. The family remains without any
information about his safety or whereabouts. These seven Indian nationals, who
were simply pursuing their livelihood at sea to support their families back home,
now face grave danger in foreign waters.
It is deeply concerning that these citizens remain unaccounted for more
than two weeks after the attack. The situation has created immense distress for
the family, particularly for the concerned families, like the parents of Mr. Asif Ali
from my Constituency, who are desperate for any news about their son. The lack
of communication and visible action from our authorities has only compounded
03.04.2025 1346
their suffering.
I urge the Government to take immediate steps to resolve this humanitarian
crisis. Our diplomatic missions should be activated without any delay to establish
contact with the affected seafarers and coordinate their safe return.
I urge the Government that a robust mechanism must be implemented to
better protect Indian seafarers working in high-risk zones. I request the
Government to take specific measures, including establishing a special task force
to monitor such cases and provide regular updates to the anxious families. The
safety and well-being of our citizens abroad must be treated as a priority.
I humbly request the Government, especially the External Affairs Ministry,
to act promptly and to ensure these seven Indians are brought back home safely
at the earliest.
SHRI PARVATAGOUDA CHANDANAGOUDA GADDIGOUDAR (BAGALKOT):
Madam, I am privileged and take pride in introducing the very beginning of Hindu
temple architecture in the Historic sites of Aihole - Badami - Pattadakal. Referred
as a cradle of Hindu temple architecture, temples complex of Aihole, dating back
to the 4th Century A.D. is the oldest known Hindu temples in the world, and also
known as the laboratory for Hindu temple architecture. The grandeous 130
temples complex of Aihole in a single square kilometre was considered the
primary and secondary schooling in temple architecture.
The world famous Badami cave temples of the 6th century took 12 years to
carve out from a single rock-cut, worthy of coronation arena for Pulikesan II of the
glorious Chalukya Empire, which ruled almost all of peninsula India.
03.04.2025 1347
The group of monuments at Pattadakal in Karnataka was declared as a
UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987. However, Badami's extraordinary temple
complexes, parallel to none in rock-cut temple architecture deserve to have been
included in this UNESCO list long back.
Therefore, Madam, through you, I would like to bring this important matter
to the notice of the hon. Union Minister of Culture and request him to make
renewed efforts for this important historical site of Badami's temple complexes in
Karnataka to be included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site List.

अनाव(cid:212)यक कं डीशन के तौर, िसफा(cid:229)रश(cid:332) के तौर पर उनके गेह(cid:243)ं क(cid:236) तौल कराई जा रही ह ै । मेरी आपस े

DR. JAYANTA KUMAR ROY (JALPAIGURI): Thank you, Madam Chairperson,
for giving me this opportunity to speak in this august House.
Madam, I rise to highlight the dire condition of tea garden workers and
small tea growers in North Bengal, including my constituency, Jalpaiguri. Many
workers face irregular wage payments with some tea estates, such as Manabari
Tea Estate in Malbazar sub-division defaulting on five wage cycles. In several tea
gardens, wages are as low as Rs. 150 per day. Adding to their plight, although
the provident fund contributions are deducted from their salaries, they are not
being deposited in their PF accounts, leaving the workers with financial insecurity.
While only a few major companies have seen a substantial growth and
continued to regulate the entire market, many others are struggling. As a result,
03.04.2025 1350
several tea estates are being leased out to other private entities, which often
neglect workers’ rights, and this further aggravates the crisis. Meanwhile, the
West Bengal Government’s move to commercialise tea estate land threatens to
displace thousands of workers and destabilize the industry.
I urge the Central Government to take immediate steps to ensure timely
wage payments, mandate cost audits for transparency, address the issue of PF
non-deposit, and explore the feasibility of a Minimum Support Price for tea
leaves.
We must remember that protecting the livelihoods of workers and small tea
growers is crucial. … (Interruptions)

SHRI ARVIND GANPAT SAWANT (MUMBAI SOUTH): Hon’ble Chairperson,
Maharashtra state was formed on 1st May, 1960, and we got the statehood after
the sacrifice of 106 martyrs. But, at that time many districts like Belgaon,
Karwar, Nipani, Bhalki, Bidar etc. did not included in Maharashtra and since
then, the people residing there are continuously protesting.
Therefore, injustice has been done to Marathi people. Hon’ble Balasaheb
Thakre had started agitations against it in 1969. Some people died even that
time also.
Then, Hon’ble Atal Behari Vajpayeeji took an initiative and invited the
Chief Minister of both the states. But, Karnataka Chief Minister did not come. A
case is pending in Supreme Court of India since 2004 regarding it, but no justice
has been delivered yet
Now, Marathi speaking people are being oppressed by the Government of
Karnataka. If somebody protests, the government declare him/her as a outlaw or
ex communicated.
One person named Subham Shelke, who contested Assembly elections
recently, is banned and declared Tadipaar (out law).
Hence, I would like to request you to impose the President’s Rule over
these disputed districts of Belgaon, Karwar, Nipani, Bhalki, Bidar so that these
Marathi speaking people should live a respectful and peaceful life there.
Thank you.

ADV. DEAN KURIAKOSE (IDUKKI): Thank you, Madam, for giving me this
opportunity. I would like to raise an important matter related to the State of
Kerala. The wages for NREGA workers are pending, which is causing significant
hardship for them. For the last three months, they have not received their wages.
In the case of unskilled workers, Rs. 578.12 crore and material costs of Rs.
57.31 crore are pending due to the non-revision of the labour budget of Kerala
State. After December, we utilised almost 90 percent of our allocated budget.
However, the Union Government is not revising the labour budget, and I request
that this be taken into consideration.
Additionally, the National Mobile Monitoring System is creating more
trouble for our poor workers. How can women above 55 years old walk from one
point to another? Before the information board, they have to take a picture, but
they cannot walk more than three kilometres. My constituency is a hilly area,
making it even more challenging for them to move from one place to another.
Therefore, I urge the Government to kindly take this into consideration.
Furthermore, wage displacement is a critical issue. The hon. Minister
stated on this floor that this problem would be resolved before April 10th. So, I
urge the Government to kindly disburse the pending wages for NREGA workers.
Thank you.

SHRI KARTI P. CHIDAMBARAM (SIVAGANGA): Madam, our banking sector
has become like a low-cost airline, where you have to pay to check in your bag, to
recline your seat, or even to get a sandwich or a bottle of water.
Our banking sector is now charging for even basic services. If you want to
withdraw your own money from an ATM, you have to pay a charge. If you want to
get an SMS for your balance, you have to pay a charge. If you want to get a
cheque book, you have to pay a charge. The most cruel thing is, if your deposit
drops below a minimum balance, they impose a penalty. As it is with this
economy, every family is struggling and every individual is struggling. When their
money dips in a bank balance, the bank charges a minimum balance fee.
I urge the Finance Ministry and the RBI to rationalise this and ensure that
they become customer-friendly. It is not very far away from a day when a bank
will start charging people if they come to a branch and want to talk to a person.
They are charging for everything that is basic and taken for granted, and this
must be rationalised. Thank you very much.

SHRI C. N. ANNADURAI (TIRUVANNAMALAI): Madam Chairperson,
Vanakkam. Tiruvannamalai and Tirupathur districts are situated in my
Tiruvannamalai parliamentary constituency. There are no big industries in this
area. Agriculture is the prime profession. Coconut cultivation has been affected
very much for the last 2 years. Particularly black headed caterpillar and white flies
affect the yield of coconut to a large extent. I submitted a letter to the Hon. Union
Minister of Agriculture in this regard and he assured to take necessary and
prompt action by sending two Teams of Experts to the affected places. But till
now, these insects affecting coconut cultivation are not controlled in my area. I
urge upon the Union Government to issue pesticides or by importing pesticides to
control these pests affecting coconut trees and their cultivation. I urge the Union
Government to safeguard coconut cultivation and coconut growing farmers of
Tiruvannamalai constituency. Thank you.

SHRI TEJASVI SURYA (BANGALORE SOUTH): Madam, the Congress
Governments in Telangana and Karnataka seem to have declared a war on the
natural heritage, forests, and the environment of Telangana and Karnataka. In
Hyderabad, 400 acres of forest land is being cleared overnight using JCBs and
bulldozers at the behest of the real estate mafia in Hyderabad to sell the 400
acres forest land to build an IT park.
In Karnataka, under the pressure of the Congress leadership, more
specifically the former MP of Wayanad and the present MP of Wayanad, the
Karnataka Government is considering opening Bandipur forest for night traffic
coming from Kerala, affecting the sensitive ecological forest area of Bandipur in
Karnataka.
03.04.2025 1362
I urge, through you, Madam, the Central Government and the Minister of
Environment and Forests to immediately take cognisance of this attack on the
forest land in Telangana, in Hyderabad, and in Karnataka and prevent this
destruction of our ecology.

SHRI SHAFI PARAMBIL (VADAKARA): Thank you, Madam for this opportunity.
I rise today to address a pressing concern that strikes at the heart of our
democracy – freedom of expression. The recent attacks on the Malayalam film
L2: Empuraan, starring the legend of Indian film Mohanlal and directed by none
other than Prithviraj Sukumaran, written by one and only Murli Gopi, by the
members of Sangh parivar, BJP leaders, and even a Central Minister, expose a
dangerous trend of intolerance that we cannot ignore.
Who decides what is acceptable expression? If you allow mob sentiment or
political agenda to dictate art, we risk losing our cultural soul. Their families are
attacked, and an actor like Mohanlal is forced to write an apology. We cannot
accept this. Freedom of expression must be upheld.
I would urge this House to reaffirm our commitment to free speech. Let
‘Empuraan’ be a wake-up call, not a warning of suppression but a testament to
resilience.

03.04.2025 1363
SHRIMATI PRATIMA MONDAL (JAYNAGAR): Thank you, Madam.
I would like to draw the attention of this House regarding the increasing
exploitation of bank employees in the country. The bank employees are burdened
with excessive work load, extended hours of work, and mounting pressure to
meet unachievable targets. Bank personnel have tirelessly served the nation
even during crises like the COVID-19 pandemic.
The Indian Banks Association has already approved the implementation of
a five-day banking week. Yet, the Government has taken no steps to enforce it
despite bodies like RBI, insurance companies, and other Government
organizations following five-day work week. So, it is only fair that their demand for
better working conditions is met, which is a demand on humanitarian grounds.
I would urge the Finance Minister to take immediate action and implement
the five-day banking week without further delay. Thank you, Madam.

DR. PRABHA MALLIKARJUN (DAVANAGERE): Hon. Chairperson, Madam,
thank you for giving me the opportunity to talk on the rising MRP of
pharmaceuticals. I wish to draw your attention to the recent decision by the
National Pharmaceutical Pricing Authority allowing 50 per cent price increase for
eight essential medicines used in the treatment of asthma, glaucoma,
03.04.2025 1368
thalassemia, tuberculosis and mental health disorders. This decision will
significantly raise the cost of formulations of life-saving drugs impacting millions of
patients across the country, particularly those from the economically weaker
sections. While it is important to ensure the viability of pharmaceutical
manufacturing, the Government must also consider the affordability of essential
medicines for common citizens. Therefore, I urge the Ministry of Chemicals and
Fertilizers to take urgent steps to prevent undue financial burden on patients and
ensure that essential medicines remain affordable to all.
SHRI TANGELLA UDAY SRINIVAS (KAKINADA): Hon. Chairperson, Madam, I
stand before this esteemed House today to bring to your attention an urgent and
heartfelt request for the inclusion of the Government General Hospital, Kakinada
under the Pradhan Mantri Swasthya Suraksha Yojana. The GGH, Kakinada is a
decades-old institution and has been a lifeline for the people across five
neighbouring districts - East Godavari, West Govadari, Alluri Sitharamraju,
Anakapalle, and Kakinada itself. Beyond serving as a critical healthcare provider,
it also functions as a teaching hospital for the Rangaraya Medical College which
is shaping the next generation of medical professionals. In the past year alone,
the GGH, Kakinada handled over eight lakh out-patients, 70,000 in-patients, and
performed more than 45,000 surgeries and conducted nearly seven lakh lab
tests. These figures highlight the number of patients who rely on this institution for
saving their lives. However, despite its critical role, the GGH, Kakinada is
struggling. While it has speciality departments like Cardiology, Neurosurgery, and
Oncology, many of them remain severely under-resourced. The hospital is
03.04.2025 1369
grappling with inadequate infrastructure, outdated equipment and staff shortage.
The gap between the rising healthcare needs and available facilities is putting
immense pressure on both patients and medical staff and making urgent
intervention necessary. Once a leader in cancer treatment in this region, GGH,
Kakinada has suffered decades of neglect. I firmly believe this is a cause worth
fighting for. I will personally meet the hon. Minister to present a detailed proposal
and push for urgent action.


03.04.2025 1372
relocated to Vijayawada in the last 15 years. A small loco running depot in
Vijayawada has now become a big one at the cost of Kazipet.

now 526 against the sanctioned number of 709. Against this background, again
the Railways sanctioned 184 new postings to Kazipet loco running station.

1100 employees are already working at Vijayawada.

KUMARI SUDHA R. (MAYILADUTHURAI): Madam Chairperson, the National
Medical Council has announced the year’s schedule for post-graduate NEET
examination. It is to be held on 5th June. But why is it being held in two shifts? No
equality can be achieved if one examination is held in two separate shifts. Last
year, when you introduced the two-shift system, there was an uproar among the
students as the second shift question papers were considered much tougher than
the first shift question paper. Also, the National Medical Council did not release
two separate results showing shift-wise performance. It was an admission that if
the results were shown separately, it would have exposed the fact that one shift
had an easy question paper and the other shift had a tough question paper. Are
the Union government and the National Medical Council so inefficient that they
cannot have one simultaneous question paper for two lakh students? If you
cannot hold a simultaneous examination for two lakh students, why are you
talking about one nation, one election for 96 crore people in India?
I would request, through you, the Union Government to kindly have one
simultaneous post-graduate NEET examination. Thank you.

and the welfare programmes, aimed for upliftment of the weaker sections, could
not be implemented in the absence of Census data. Not only that, in order to go
for delimitation of parliamentary constituencies, it is important to have up-to-date
Census data. This Census is also important for implementing the 33 percent
reservation for Women in Legislative Bodies. I therefore urge the Government to
start the process for enumeration of Census immediately without any further
delay. I also make a request to include caste–based census along with the
enumeration of regular Census by the Government. Thank you.

SHRI ANTO ANTONY (PATHANAMTHITTA): Madam Chairperson, I stand today
to highlight the serious challenges faced by ASHA workers who are the backbone
of India’s healthcare system. Despite their crucial role, they continue to struggle
due to poor working conditions and low pay.
I request the Government to take urgent steps to declare ASHA workers as
last-grade Government employees across the country so that they will get reward
for their work. The ASHA workers handle multiple responsibilities, leading to
heavy workload and health risks. Many suffer from malnutrition, anaemia, and
other diseases due to lack of proper support. The shocking fact is that their
current incentive is Rs. 2,000 per month, which is highly inadequate and failing to
reflect the critical nature of their services. Also, many are forced to spend from
their own pockets for work-related expenses, pushing them further into financial
03.04.2025 1378
distress.
Madam, in Kerala, the ASHA workers played a crucial role in effectively
managing the COVID-19 pandemic. However, the inadequate incentives provided
by both the State and Central Governments have left them struggling to make
ends meet despite their vital contributions to public health.

SHRI ROBERT BRUCE C. (TIRUNELVELI): Madam, in my Constituency
Tirunelveli in Tamil Nadu, majority of people are farmers and they depend only on
agriculture. The wild boar, that is, wild pigs, are coming from the forest in groups
and staying in agri-fields and destroying crops like banana, paddy and all other
crops and thereby, causing huge loss to the farmers.
Many times, they have attacked farmers and caused grievous injuries also.
The Union Government is not giving any compensation. Wild boar is included in
Schedule III of the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972. It is a Central Act. If the farmers
try to prevent the boars, the Forest Department is registering cases against them.
Hence, I request the Union Government to remove wild boar from Schedule
III of the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.
Thank you, Madam.

regularly.

cost of treatment. That is why, I request to the Central Government to kindly take

DR. SHARMILA SARKAR (BARDHAMAN PURBA): Thank you very much,
Madam, for giving me the opportunity to speak on a very important yet neglected
issue, which is mental health awareness.
The prevalence of mental health disorders in India has surged, especially
after COVID-19, affecting about 15 per cent of the population. Individuals with
mental health issues face considerable personal distress, hindering their ability to
maintain relationships, pursue education, and engage socially. These challenges
impact communities and the nation by reducing productivity through absenteeism
and diminished work efficiency. Ultimately, this hinders the economic growth of
the nation.
Socially, the stigma and discrimination surrounding mental health
compromise individuals’ ability to seek help, leading to delays in diagnosis and
treatment, and perpetuating their suffering. This significant mental health burden
ultimately affects the nation’s growth. What steps has the Government taken to
increase awareness?

SHRI TAMILSELVAN THANGA (THENI): Madam Chairperson, Vanakkam. I
wish to bring to you king notice regarding the order issued by the Reserve Bank
of India recently. It has been the practice that farmers get loans every year from
the nationalised banks of our country. These loans are to be paid back every
year. Only the interest part can be paid and the actual loan can be renewed for
the next year as a new loan. This Jewel loan can be extended for a year. But the
Reserve Bank of India has issued a recent Order stating that only if both the
Principal and Interest portions of the loan are paid in full, you can avail new loans.
As a result of this recent Order of RBI, the farmers are perturbed and affected. I
therefore humbly request that this Order of Reserve Bank of India should be
cancelled and the farmers may be allowed to repay only the interest part and get
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
03.04.2025 1385
their loans renewed for the consecutive year as it was done in the past. Thank
you.

SHRI BHASKAR MURLIDHAR BHAGARE (DINDORI): Hon’ble Chairperson,
in our country, farming is considered as a backbone of our economy. Farmers
work relentlessly in the farms to fulfill the needs of our country as well as the
entire world. Urea is essential for farming and it is being provided at subsidized
rates by the government. Industrial usage Urea is also available at higher price.
But, the farmers are facing the acute shortage of Urea and they have to
procure it at costlier rates from open market. There is a factory in my constituency
called Godrej Agrovet which manufactures fodder and feed for cattle. Around 90
MT of Urea have been seized from this company in a raid.
Subsidized Urea is not available in market in sufficient quantity and hence,
the farmers are bound to procure it by paying more money. But, on the other
hand, it is being supplied to big companies. So, what is the link between these
two cases? How is it happening? From which part of India, it is being supplied?
Government should investigate it properly.
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Marathi.
03.04.2025 1386

SHRI MALVINDER SINGH KANG (ANANDPUR SAHIB): Thank you, Madam
Chairperson. Through you, I want to bring to the attention of the Govt. an
important matter. In 2019, the Government had decided to release the Sikhs
imprisoned in jails. A notification had also been issued. Six long years have
passed but the Sikhs languishing in jails have not yet been released. They are
political prisoners lying in jails for the last 25 to 30 years. They have even
completed their terms of sentence.
Madam Chairperson, some of them are unwell. Keeping in view this fact
and from a humanitarian point of view, I urge upon the Government to release
these imprisoned Sikhs. The Government of India had promised to release them
in 2019 via a notification. It will help heal wounds of Punjab. Thank you.
ADV. FRANCIS GEORGE (KOTTAYAM): Thank you very much, Madam. The
Moonilavu Panchayat is the only SC/ST Panchayat in my Parliament
constituency. The Kuda Kallu bridge connects this panchayat with the outside
world. This bridge got heavily damaged during the 2001 floods. Even though the
State Government got the Disaster Management Fund, they have failed to
construct the bridge. The local people have to take a detour of 20 kilometres to
reach the settlement. I request the hon. Minister of Tribal Affairs to grant funds to
reconstruct the road and the bridge at the earliest. Thank you very much.

SHRI SHREYAS M. PATEL (HASSAN): Thank you, hon. Chairperson. I would
like to draw the attention of the Ministry of Tourism and Culture to the urgent
renovation work of Belur Sri Chennakeshava Temple and Shankargouda Sri
Bahubali Gomateshwara Temple in my constancy. They are the symbol of our
rich history.
The Chennakeshava Temple in Belur showcases the incredible
craftsmanship of our Hoysala dynasty which is a UNESCO recognized world
heritage site. The Shankargouda Temple is well known for the monolithic statue
of Lord Gomateshwara, also known as Bahubali, and is renowned for the
Mahamastakabhisheka. However, these heritage sites are facing neglect, with
crumbling walls that threaten their beauty. This is an urgent matter and, through
you, I would like to urge the Government to take immediate action. Thank you,
Madam.

SHRI DURAI VAIKO (TIRUCHIRAPPALLI): Hon. Chairperson Madam,
Vanakkam. We are very well aware that only by safeguarding the fishing industry
which is the only livelihood of our fishermen, we can safeguard our fishermen
community. On this basis, fulfilling the expectations of entire Tamil Nadu, with the
support of all the political parties of Tamil Nadu, Hon. Chief Minister of Tamil
Nadu Annan Thiru M.K. Stalin has passed a resolution in the State Legislative
Assembly of Tamil Nadu for retrieving Katchatheevu. I thank the Government of
Tamil Nadu at this juncture. Hon Prime Minister Thiru Narendra Modi will be going
to Sri Lanka on an Official Visit very soon. Sri Lanka has not followed the terms of
the agreement of 1973 between India and Sri Lanka on Katchatheevu. Therefore
on behalf of Marumalarchi Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, MDMK, I urge upon the
Union Government that the Agreement of 1973, about ceding away of
Katchatheevu to Sri Lanka, should be cancelled and determined efforts should be
undertaken to reach a permanent solution in retrieving Katchatheevu in the
interest of fishermen of Tamil Nadu.

SHRIMATI SANGEETA KUMARI SINGH DEO (BOLANGIR): Madam, the issue
which I am raising concerns the environmental and health hazards caused by the
improper disposal of phosphogypsum waste by Paradeep Phosphates Limited
(PPL) in Jagatsinghpur district of Odisha. A large accumulation of this toxic
industrial by-product near Chanakana village has resulted in acidic water leaching
into nearby agricultural fields severely impacting soil quality, crop yield, and local
biodiversity. The farmers in the region are facing significant losses and
contamination poses serious health risks to the local population.
Despite PPL's stated commitments to waste management and recycling,
the current situation highlights serious lapses in implementation
I urge the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change to conduct
an immediate investigation and take stringent and exemplary measures to ensure
proper disposal and management of industrial waste.

SHRI LAVU SRIKRISHNA DEVARAYALU (NARASARAOPET): Madam, this is
a very important issue relating to my State. It is related to the seafood sector of
Andhra Pradesh.
Madam, 11 per cent of our State’s GDP, that is the GDP of Andhra
Pradesh, is related to the seafood sector, seafood exports. Eight lakh farmers
directly or indirectly get benefited by the seafood exports. 3.5 billion dollars of
exports happen every year from Andhra Pradesh in seafoods. Last time, when
we were burning the midnight oil, there was a tariff that has been imposed on
India. There is 26 per cent of tariff that has been imposed on India, whereas the
competing country, which is Ecuador, which competes with us in the seafood
sector is only limited to 10 per cent. So, the conclusion is this. I know that we
have so much confidence on the Prime Minister that he will be able to help this
sector. But I request the Commerce Minister to look at this very seriously
because this is a very important sector for Andhra Pradesh. There are eight lakh
farmers who are involved in this. The economy is also very much intertwined with
this.
So, I request the Commerce Ministry to look into this in a positive way.

Chairperson, Amrit Yojana worth Rs 302 crore was sanctioned for my city

water regularly. But, the people are still suffering and the drinking water is being
supplied once in a fortnight.

facing water scarcity in this area. For Darwha city, Rs 34.34 crore was spent
under Maharashtra Suvarna Jayanti Nagarotthan Abhiyan for drinking water

sanctioned worth Rs 43 crore for Digras city too and it is also incomplete.

incomplete and people are also suffering a lot due to poor implementation of
these schemes. So, an In-depth enquiry should be initiated to find out the reasons

punished for this inordinate delay.

CAPTAIN BRIJESH CHOWTA (DAKSHINA KANNADA): Thank you, Madam. I
want to raise the issue faced by veteran soldiers in availing of disability pension
once they are out of service.
A soldier is never out of duty, but circumstances may lead them to retire,
especially in cases of injuries. When such cases occur, we should collectively
support the family in obtaining the disability pension. Numerous veterans have
reported an overly complex administrative process. Once the documents are
submitted and the reports are provided, their applications are often stalled by
repeated requests for additional documents and assessments.
Many court cases, litigations, and court appearances are currently taking
place. Therefore, I urge the Ministry to ensure that ex-servicemen do not have to
endure this. The Ministry should establish a single-window system to enhance
accountability and ensure a standardized process so that veterans do not face
these challenges in availing of their disability pension. Thank you.

03.04.2025 1409
SHRI V. K. SREEKANDAN (PALAKKAD): Respected Madam, the drug menace
is creating a dangerous social problem in Kerala. Kerala is now flooded with
drugs which is available at every nook and corner of the State. It is reported that
drugs worth over Rs. 25,000 are crore reaching Kerala from other States
including Gujarat and abroad.
Due to this drug addiction, especially among the youth segment, crime has
increased in the State substantially. If we are not going to take any strong step,
the future of our young generation of Kerala will be at peril because of use of this
drugs.
I strongly urge the Government to implement stringent oversight by all
relevant agencies to effectively combat drug and narcotic issues, thereby
safeguarding the youth and ensuring a brighter future for the next generation of
Kerala. Thank you.

DR. M. K. VISHNU PRASAD (CUDDALORE): Hon. Madam Chairperson,
Vanakkam. In my Cuddalore parliamentary constituency, there is a big shipping
harbour. This Shipping harbour, which was successfully operational till some time
ago, has now been closed. The workers dependent on this harbour were very
much affected due to its closure. Under the Sagarmala Programme, this
Cuddalore shipping harbour should be opened once again in the interests of
workers of this area. There was a facility for anchoring at least two Ships in this
 English translation of the speech originally delivered in Tamil.
03.04.2025 1413
harbour. Due to insufficiency in its operations, this harbour has been closed. I
urge that the activities of Cuddalore harbour should be expanded further. Through
this, there will an opportunity for setting up of Industries in and around this
harbour besides providing livelihood for thousands of workers of this area. This
will also generate more income to Cuddalore district as well. I therefore urge upon
the Union Government to revive and expand the Cuddalore shipping harbour with
adequate allocation of funds. Thank you.

(i) Need to develop wetland recognized cities in the line of Indore and
Udaipur to promote sustainable development of cities in the country
SHRI MUKESHKUMAR CHANDRAKAANT DALAL (SURAT): Indore in Madhya
Pradesh and Udaipur in Rajasthan have been included in the list of 31 wetland
(moisture or marshy land area) recognized cities of the world. This is an
extraordinary feat for both the states as well as the country. This recognition to
both the cities reflects India's strong commitment towards sustainable
development and nurturing harmony between nature and urban development.
This achievement will inspire every citizen of the country to work towards creating
greener, cleaner and more eco-friendly urban spaces. Prime Minister has
congratulated both the cities on their inclusion in this list. There is tremendous
potential in India to develop more such cities in line with Indore & Udaipur. Surat
could be one of them. The Government should think of it earliest. Such actions
would promote sustainable development of cities in India.

(iv) Need to expedite laying of CNG pipeline work and supply of CNG to
petrol pumps in Udupi district in Karnataka
SHRI KOTA SRINIVASA POOJARY (UDUPI CHIKMAGALUR): I urge the
Minister of Petroleum and Natural Gas to address the severe Compressed
Natural Gas (CNG) shortage in Udupi district, Karnataka, which is causing
immense hardship for auto rickshaw drivers and owners. The CNG pumps in the
area have kilometre-long queues, forcing people to wait for hours. Despite my
03.04.2025 1420
repeated efforts to bring this issue to the attention of the Minister of Petroleum
and Natural Gas, the problem persists. Therefore, I request the Union Minister of
Petroleum and Natural Gas to complete the pending pipeline work and provide
CNG to all petrol pumps in Udupi district, thereby resolving the CNG crisis in the
area.
(v) Need to launch a time-bound National Non-communicable Disease
Control Mission and regulation of Branched-Chain Amino-Acids
(BCAA) supplements
SHRI NAVEEN JINDAL (KURUKSHETRA): I like to draw the attention of the
Government to the issue of urgent need for a National NCD (Non-Communicable
Disease) Control Mission and Regulation of BCAA (Branched-Chain Amino
Acids) Supplements. According to the WHO, approximately 5.8 million people in
India constituting about 63% of all deaths-die each year due to non-
communicable diseases (NCDs) like heart ailments, cancer, diabetes, and lung
diseases. The situation not only adds strain to our health system but may also
weaken our biggest advantage-the demographic dividend-if our youth succumb to
chronic illnesses. As per ICMR report, indiscriminate use of protein supplements
containing BCAA (Branched-Chain Amino Acids) and steroids often consumed
without medical advice can greatly increase the risk of diabetes, liver damage,
and heart disease among the youth. Lured by gyms and social media influences,
young people are using these substances, potentially triggering a public health
crisis in the future. Hence, I urge the Government to launch a time-bound
National NCD Control Mission similar to TB and polio programs, instituting real-
03.04.2025 1421
time data dashboards at the district level, providing mobile screening vans, and
making wellness programs mandatory in schools and workplaces. I also suggest
that the Government establish a robust regulatory framework to strictly monitor
the sale, advertising, and content related to such supplements and integrate this
into the national strategy for NCD prevention which will ensure that our youth
receive accurate information and remain safeguarded from these health risks.
(vi) Need for a multipurpose major irrigation project including construction
of dam at Gambhira Darha in Boudh district, Odisha
SHRI SUKANTA KUMAR PANIGRAHI (KANDHAMAL): I want to request the
Government to consider the proposal for Multipurpose Major Irrigation (MIP) and
construct a dam at Gambhira Darha near Gurjimunda, Boudh district, Odisha.
(vii) Need to create ecological parks in all the districts including Kalahandi
and Naupada districts of Odisha
SHRIMATI MALVIKA DEVI (KALAHANDI): I would like to request our
honourable Minister of Environment, Forest and Climate Change to please
consider making and introducing ecological parks in all districts of Odisha
covering 10-20 acres of land. This park will not only improve the environment and
be a place for people to admire and appreciate beauty of nature but will also help
climate change and help us to live a healthier life. A lot of Government lands are
turning into big dump and garbage yards which is very sad for our children but
this will also teach children from young age how important our environment is for
our future generations to have a good and healthy life. They will also help in
learning to preserve our Mother Nature and importance of planting trees. Please
03.04.2025 1422
make two at least to start with in my aspirational district of Kalahandi and
Nuapada in my Kalahandi constituency.

(xi) Need for road connectivity to all the unconnected habitations under
PMGSY in Mahbubnagar Parliamentary Constituency, Telangana
SHRIMATI D. K. ARUNA (MAHBUBNAGAR): I would like to bring to the notice
of Government the dire need to lay roads connecting from unconnected
Habitations to Gram Panchayats under PMGSY in identified areas in all 7
Assembly Constituencies which falls in my Mahabubnagar Parliamentary
Constituency in Telangana State. I would like to state that totally, there are 5
districts consisting of Mahabubnagar, KV Ranga Reddy, Narayanpet,
Wanaparthy, Vikarabad, and 7 Assembly Constituencies Kodangal (CMs
Constituency), Jadcherla, Devarakadra, Shadnagar, Maktal, Narayanpet,
Mahabubnagar having in my Mahabubnagar Parliamentary Constituency in
Telangana State. As we all are aware, Rural Road Connectivity is not only a key
component of Rural Development by promoting access to economic and social
services and thereby generating increased agricultural incomes and productive
employment opportunities in India and as a result, it is a key ingredient in
ensuring sustainable poverty reduction of the people living in habitations and to
develop such habitations so that the fruits of various products will reach from
habitations to Gram Panchayats to Mandal Headquarters to District Headquarters
to provide proper connectivity. Notwithstanding the efforts made, over the years,
at the State and Central levels, through different Programmes including PMGSY
which is 100% Centrally Sponsored Scheme, many Habitations in my
Constituency is still not connected by All-weather roads to the eligible
unconnected Habitations in the rural areas with a population of 500 persons and
03.04.2025 1425
above in Plain areas and also to achieve the goal of 'Viksit Bharat 2047'.
Whenever I tour in my Constituency, the local people in such habitations remind
me to take up this issue at the Ministry level on priority basis. Hence, I request the
Hon'ble Minister of Rural Development to kindly intervene in the matter and take
steps to lay roads connecting from unconnected Habitations to Gram Panchayats
under PMGSY or under any other scheme in identified areas in all 7 Assembly
Constituencies which falls in my Mahabubnagar Parliamentary Constituency in
Telangana State.
